questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
With regard to the National Green Hydrogen
Mission, consider the following statements:
I. 	 It aims to develop 5 million metric tonnes
of green hydrogen production capacity per
annum by 2030.
II. 	 It provides financial incentives for domestic
manufacturing of electrolysers.
III.  	India’s first indigenously-built green
hydrogen plant was commissioned at Kandla
Port.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
India has set an ambitious target of becoming **energy independent by 2047 and achieving** **Net Zero emissions by 2070.** To reach these goals, the country is focusing on expanding the use of renewable energy across all economic sectors.
● The **National** **Green** **Hydrogen** **Mission,** approved by the Union Cabinet in 2022, aims to position India as a leading global producer and supplier of Green Hydrogen.
● By 2030, the Mission envisions developing a green hydrogen production capacity of **at least 5** **million metric tonnes per annum**, supported by around 125 gigawatts of additional renewable energy capacity. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Under the **Strategic Interventions for Green Hydrogen Transition Programme (SIGHT)**, the Mission will provide **two distinct financial incentive mechanisms,** one targeting **domestic manufacturing of** **electrolysers** and the other focused on **production of Green Hydrogen**. **So, statement II is correct.**
○ The Mission will also support pilot projects in emerging end-use sectors and production pathways. ○ The **regions** with the potential for **large-scale production** and/or **utilization of Hydrogen** will be identified and developed as **Green Hydrogen Hubs**.
● India has achieved a major milestone in its clean energy journey with the commissioning of the country’s **first Make-in-India Green Hydrogen Plant** in the **port sector** at **Kandla, Gujarat**. It has become the **first Indian port** to host a **megawatt-scale indigenous green hydrogen facility**. **So, statement III is** **correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
**Key Facts About Green Hydrogen:**
● Hydrogen is classified based on its production method. **Green hydrogen** is produced through the electrolysis of water using electricity generated from renewable sources, with its carbon intensity determined by the share of renewables in the electricity mix.
● **The objectives of the National Green Hydrogen Mission**: ○ Making India a leading producer and supplier of Green Hydrogen in the world ○ Creation of export opportunities for Green Hydrogen and its derivatives ○ Reduction in dependence on imported fossil fuels and feedstock ○ Development of indigenous manufacturing capabilities ○ Attracting investment and business opportunities for the industry ○ Creating opportunities for employment and economic development ○ Supporting R&D projects
● **The mission outcomes projected by 2030 are:** ○ Over Rs. Eight lakh crore in total investments ○ Creation of over Six lakh jobs ○ Cumulative reduction in fossil fuel imports over Rs. One lakh crore ○ Abatement of nearly 50 MMT of annual greenhouse gas emissions
**Relevance:** The Prime Minister of India has hailed India's first Make-in-India Green Hydrogen Plant at Kandla port in Gujarat as a landmark step towards sustainability and Net-Zero emissions.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
‘Kodali Karuppur’ and ‘Kovai Kora’ are the
types of:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
‘Kodali Karuppur’ and ‘Kovai Kora’ are types of traditional handloom sarees.
● The **Kodali Karuppur silk saree**, a luxury garment once patronised by the **Maratha kings of Thanjavur**, derives its name from Karuppur, a village near Kumbakonam. The handwoven silk saris, dhotis, and furnishings of Karuppur combined **hand painting**, **block printing**, and **brocade weaving**, and were popular up to the **19th century**.
● The **Kovai Kora cotton saree**, recognised with a **Geographical** **Indication (GI)** tag is a distinctive textile that blends **silk** and **cotton**. The product is closely associated with the **Sirumugai** area, where the **Devanga** **community** plays a predominant role in its weaving and is regarded as the pioneer in making it. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**The GI-tagged Thirubhuvanam Silk Saree of Tamil Nadu**: It is a GI-tagged traditional wedding saree, is about 18 feet long and 4 feet wide with a one-side border. It is woven using filature silk in both warp and weft, giving it high shine, uniformity, and lustre. Made with the unique **Porai Ilupu** **technique** (different from the Pitni work of Kancheepuram), its speciality lies in the **Visiri** **Madippu folding style** resembling a palm fan. With intricate borders, motifs, and a continuous woven pallu, the saree’s warp tension provides natural stiffness and better drape.
**Relevance:** Decline in handloom weaving despite higher market value highlights the threat to traditional skills, low wages of artisans, and challenges in preserving India’s cultural heritage.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
With reference to Cable Internet and Satellite
Internet, consider the following statements:
I. 	 Unlike Cable Internet, Satellite Internet offers
internet access regardless of location.
II. 	 Unlike Cable Internet, Satellite Internet offers
lower latency.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Satellite internet can provide access **regardless of location**, as its signal is beamed from satellites and can be received anywhere with a clear view of the sky. This makes it useful in rural or remote areas without cable or fiber connectivity. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● Satellite internet has **higher** **latency** compared to cable connections because the signal travels a longer distance between Earth and satellites, leading to slight delays. Cable internet offers **lower latency**. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● **Cable internet is more affordable** than satellite internet. Satellite internet is generally more expensive due to installation requirements and the technology involved, though it offers wider coverage.
● **Cable internet is faster** than satellite internet, with speeds up to 2,000 Mbps in some cases. Satellite internet speeds are adequate for most everyday uses but are generally lower than cable speeds. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Differences Between Cable Internet and Satellite Internet:**
**Cable Internet** **Satellite Internet**
Uses **underground coaxial cables** with copper wires wrapped in insulation to transmit data.
Uses **signals beamed from satellites** orbiting Earth, received by a **dish** and transmitted to a modem.
Common in **urban and semi-urban** **areas** where cable networks already exist.
Available **almost everywhere** with a clear view of the sky, including rural and remote areas.
Can be installed by a **professional** or through **self-installation kits**.
Requires **professional** **installation**, including setting up the dish and modem.
Often **bundled with TV packages**, making it more cost-effective.
No typical bundling with TV; cost is mainly for internet access.
Packages, providers, and prices are **not** **standardised** and vary across regions.
Pricing is generally high but more **uniform** due to fewer providers and specialised service.
**Relevance:** Jio Platforms has recently signed a joint venture with European satellite-based broadband service company SES to explore the satellite internet space.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following microorganisms
is used to degrade Polyethylene Terephthalate
(PET), the plastic most commonly used in bottles
and food packaging?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Scientists are exploring **microbial degradation** as a sustainable solution to **plastic** **pollution.** Certain **bacteria and** **fungi** produce specialised enzymes capable of breaking down the **long carbon-rich** **chains** that form plastic polymers, thereby using them as a food source.
● One of the most significant discoveries is **Ideonella sakaiensis,** identified near a plastic recycling facility in Japan. This bacterium can **completely degrade Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET)**, the most common plastic in bottles and food packaging, by breaking it into environmentally safe building blocks, which can then be metabolised by itself and other organisms.
● These microbes did not evolve this trait in response to modern plastics. Instead, scientists are repurposing **naturally existing metabolic functions**, originally meant for breaking down organic compounds, to tackle synthetic polymers. Research in plastic-polluted sites like **landfills and contaminated soils** has been especially fruitful, as prolonged exposure often fosters organisms capable of utilising plastics as a carbon source. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, scientists have been exploring microbial degradation as a solution to plastic pollution, utilising bacteria and fungi to break down plastics.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Air, Water, Noise Pollution, SLCPs, etc.
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:**
The Indian clouds are more polluted than those in
countries like China, Pakistan, Italy, and the USA.
**Statement II:**
Eastern Himalayas experience higher polluted
clouds containing toxic metals from vehicular and
industrial emissions over foothill regions.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of
the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct but Statement II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Recent research has revealed that clouds over the **Eastern Himalayas** and **Western Ghats**, once considered a source of **pure drinking water**, are now transporting **toxic heavy metals** from polluted lowland areas to fragile high-altitude ecosystems.
○ **Eastern Himalayas experience higher polluted clouds containing toxic metals like Cd, Cr,** **Cu, and Zn coming from vehicular and industrial emissions** over foothill regions. Inhalation of such polluted clouds is the potential cause of carcinogenic and non-carcinogenic diseases in India. **So, statement II is correct.** ○ Clouds acting as transport medium, carry dissolved heavy metals, posing serious health risks to people, due to carcinogenic and non-carcinogenic diseases, through skin contact, inhalation, and consumption of precipitation-collected water in high-altitude regions.
● While Indian clouds remain less polluted than those in countries like **China, Pakistan, Italy, and the** **USA**, the study challenges the belief in the **pristine nature** of mountain rainwater.  **So, statement I is not** **correct.**
● The findings show that **children** in affected regions face a **30% higher risk** of health issues than adults. **Inhalation** was identified as the major route of exposure for non-carcinogenic diseases, while skin contact, breathing polluted air, and consumption of rainwater also contribute.
● The study also noted **increased carcinogenic risk due to inhalation of dissolved chromium (Cr),** emphasizing the growing threat posed by cloudborne pollutants. **So, statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** A recent study has found that the clouds are transporting toxic heavy metals from polluted areas to fragile high-altitude ecosystems.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
In the context of public finance, what is "short
transfer"?

options.a:
Non-release of States’ tax devolution share as
per Finance Commission recommendations
options.b:
Diversion of receipts meant for the
Consolidated Fund into the Contingency
Fund of India
options.c:
Transfer of surcharge proceeds directly to
State governments without Parliamentary
approval
options.d:
Failure to transfer the full amount of cess
collected to the designated reserve funds

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In public finance, a **short transfer** refers to the situation when the **entire amount of cess or levy collected** **is not transferred from the Consolidated Fund of India (CFI)** to the **designated Reserve Funds in** **the Public Account of India**, as required by law and Parliamentary approval.
● The **Comptroller and Auditor General of India (CAG),** in its report on Union Accounts for **2022-23** **and 2023-24,** highlighted the rising contribution of cess and surcharge to the Centre’s tax revenues. Collections stood at ₹4.7 lakh crore in 2022-23 and ₹4.9 lakh crore in 2023-24, accounting for nearly 12– 14% of gross tax receipts.
● However, the CAG expressed concern that the **government did not fully utilise these levies for their** **intended purposes**. A **short transfer of ₹3.69 lakh crore** was noted till March 2024, where cess proceeds were not moved to the designated reserve funds. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** According to the recent Comptroller and Auditor General of India (CAG) report, the Centre withheld ₹3.69 lakh crore of cess collections by not transferring them to the designated reserve funds.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Wildlife Conservation (Tiger, Elephant, Lion, Rhino, Crocodile, Vulture, Tortoise, Dolphin, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Rhino species Feature
I. - Sumatran rhino Smallest of all living rhino species
II. - Javan rhino Only Asian rhino with two horns
III. Indian rhino Most threatened of all rhino species
IV. - African rhino Largest of all rhino species
How many of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
There are five species of rhinos across the world.
● **Sumatran rhinos are the smallest of the living rhinoceroses** and the **only Asian rhino with two horns.** They are covered with long hair and are more closely related to the extinct woolly rhinos than any of the other rhino species alive today. Today, the species only survives on the Indonesian islands of Sumatra and Borneo. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● **Javan rhinos are the most threatened of the five rhino** **species**, with only around 76 individuals that live only in Ujung Kulon National Park in Java, Indonesia. Javan rhinos once lived throughout northeast India and Southeast Asia. Vietnam’s last Javan rhino was poached in 2010. This species is a dusky grey color and has a **single horn**. It is very similar in appearance to the **closely-related greater one-horned rhinoceros**, but has a much smaller head and less apparent skin folds. **So, pair II is** **not correctly matched.**
● **The greater one-horned rhino (or “Indian rhino”) is the** **largest of the Asian rhino species.** The recovery of the greater one-horned rhino is among the greatest conservation success stories in Asia. Today populations have increased to around 4000 rhinos in northeastern India and the Terai grasslands of Nepal and it is listed as **Vulnerable under the IUCN Red list**. The greater one-horned rhino is identified by a single black horn and a grey-brown hide with skin folds, which gives it an armor-plated appearance. The species is solitary, except when adult males or rhinos nearing adulthood gather at wallows or to graze. **So, pair III is not correctly matched.**
● Among **black and white rhinos, black rhinos are the smaller of the two African rhino species.** Black and white rhinos can be distinguished by the shape of their lips.
○ **Black rhinos** have hooked upper lips, whereas white rhinos are characterized by a square lip. Black rhinos are browsers, rather than grazers, meaning they are **herbivores who do not feed on low-growing vegetation**, and their pointed lip helps them feed on leaves from bushes and trees. ○ They have two horns, which grow continually from the skin at their base throughout the rhino’s life (like human fingernails). The front horn is longer than the rear horn, averaging around 19 inches long. ○ **White rhinos** are the **second-largest land mammal** and **largest of all rhino species**. Their name comes from the Afrikaan’s, a West Germanic language, word “weit” which means wide and refers to the animal’s mouth. Two genetically different subspecies exist, the northern and southern white rhino, and are found in two different regions in Africa. The majority (98.8%) of the southern white rhinos occur in just four countries: South Africa, Namibia, Zimbabwe, and Kenya. **So, pair IV is** **correctly matched.** **So, only two of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Rhino numbers in India and Nepal have risen from 4,014 to 4,075, as per the recent report.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
With regard to the Stem Cells, consider the
following statements:
I. 	 Undifferentiated cells can give rise to heart
muscle cells and blood cells.
II. 	 Differentiated cells can be reprogrammed
back into the state of pluripotency.
III. 	Pluripotent cells cannot be cultured in the
lab.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Stem cells** have the remarkable **potential to renew themselves**. They can develop into many different cell types in the body during early life and growth. Researchers study many different types of stem cells.
○ When a stem cell divides, the resulting two daughter cells may be: 1) both stem cells, 2) a stem cell and a more differentiated cell, or 3) both more differentiated cells. What controls the balance between these types of divisions to maintain stem cells at an appropriate level within a given tissue is not yet well known.
● There are several main categories: the **“pluripotent”** stem cells (embryonic stem cells and induced pluripotent stem cells) and **“nonembryonic or somatic”** stem cells (commonly called **“adult”** stem cells).
● **Pluripotent stem cells have the ability to differentiate into all of the cells of the adult body**. They are **undifferentiated**; they **do not have any tissue-specific characteristics** (such as morphology or gene expression pattern) that allow them to perform specialized functions. Yet they can give rise to all of the differentiated cells in the body, such as **heart muscle cells, blood cells, and nerve cells**. **So, statement I** **is correct.**
○ On the other hand, **adult stem cells differentiate to yield the specialized cell types of the tissue** **or organ** in which they reside, and may have defining morphological features and patterns of gene expression reflective of that tissue.
● Differentiated cells, such as skin cells, can be reprogrammed back into a **pluripotent state**. Reprogramming is achieved over several weeks by forced expression of genes that are known to be master regulators of pluripotency. At the end of this process, these master regulators will remodel the expression of an entire network of genes. Features of differentiated cells will be replaced by those associated with the pluripotent state, essentially reversing the developmental process. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Pluripotent cells only exist at the early starting point of our lifetime**. Importantly, this pluripotent cell state is a transient one, which can be observed in mammalian development. We are now **capable of** **capturing some of these pluripotent cells in culture**. According to the recent progress of the imaging tools as well as embryo culture, we can monitor peri‐implantation development in vitro relatively easily and also genetics allows us to examine the gene function in a stage‐ or tissue‐specific manner. **So,** **statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Scientists are exploring ways to mimic the origins of human life without two fundamental components - sperm and egg.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
It is located on the banks of the river
Chandrabhaga, built by a ruler of the Hoysala
dynasty, and classified as a focal point of the
Warkari pilgrimage.
Which is this temple?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Vitthal Rukmini Temple** at **Pandharpur**, Maharashtra is the principal centre of worship for **Vitthal** (Vithoba), regarded as a local form of **Lord Krishna** or **Lord Vishnu**, and his consort **Rakhumai** (Rukmini).
● The temple stands on the **banks of the Chandrabhaga River**, where taking a holy dip is believed to wash away sins. The **main shrine** is a five-storey square structure with a **pyramidal shikhara** and features the statues of **Jaya** and **Vijaya** flanking Lord Vitthal.
● It was originally built by **King Vishnuvardhana** of the **Hoysala Empire** between **1108 and 1152 CE**, holds deep importance in the **Vaishnavite tradition** and is counted among the **108 Abhimana** **Kshethrams**.
● It is a focal point of the **Warkari pilgrimage**, when thousands of devotees walk to Pandharpur during **Aashadhi Ekadashi** and **Kartiki Ekadashi**.
● While its origins trace back to the 12th century, the temple’s **present structure**, largely from the **17th** **century**, showcases characteristic **Deccan architectural styles**. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** A Marathi–Hindi language row erupted at the Vitthal-Rukmini temple in Pandharpur after a devotee complained that rituals were conducted in Hindi for one non-Marathi family, despite the majority of devotees being Marathi speakers.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:**
Tropical rainfall can sometimes reduce vertical
mixing in the upper layers of the ocean.
**Statement II:**
Cold pools during tropical rain cool the ocean’s
surface and increase the transfer of heat from the
water to the air.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of
the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct but Statement II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Rain in the tropics often comes with large clouds and cold, dry air called cold pools. These **cold pools can** **actually cool the ocean’s surface by blocking sunlight and increasing the transfer of heat from the** **water to the air.** So instead of making the surface lighter, which would help mix waters in the ocean, **rain sometimes makes the surface heavier and more stable.  So, statements I and II are correct.**
● A recent study stated that focusing on the **buoyancy flux**, which combines the effects of heat and freshwater, if it is positive, the ocean surface is less stable and promotes mixing.
● During light rain (0.2-4 mm/hr), **buoyancy flux was often positive**, meaning it tends to destabilise the ocean and promote mixing. But during heavier rain, the buoyancy flux was almost always negative, meaning the ocean surface was more stable. This is because **heavy rain is usually accompanied by** **stronger cold pools that pull heat out of the ocean more effectively.**
● The study also found that **at night, rainfall was more likely to cause instability than during the day**. The study mentioned a **‘cold rain zone’**, mainly in the western Pacific and Indian Oceans, where rain was associated with more heat loss, and a **‘hot rain zone’** in the central Pacific where heat loss was less intense. These differences were tied to sea surface temperatures and the strength of the accompanying atmospheric downdrafts. **So, both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Instead of making the surface lighter, which would help mix waters in the ocean, a new study has found rain sometimes makes the surface heavier.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following shows the unique
characteristic of remaining intact throughout the
winter months despite the subzero temperature?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The actinorhizal plant **sea buckthorn** (Hippophae rhamnoides L.) of the family Elaegnaceae has the **unique characteristics to grow in marginally fertile** **soil of cold desert**. It is naturally distributed over 11,500 hectares in Ladakh region. The shrub can withstand extreme temperature from **-43°C to +40°C** and is considered drought resistant. These two characteristics make the shrub an ideal plant species to establish in cold deserts.
● Establishment of seabuckthorn plantation led to improved soil fertility and moisture conservation leading to shrub-grass community of thick forest after 4-5 years.
● Roots of seabuckthorn have **symbiotic association with bacteria belonging to genus Frankia.** The symbiotic association fixes atmospheric nitrogen in soil thereby increasing soil fertility of nutrient deficit soil in deserts. It is estimated that seabuckthorn plantations fix 180 kg nitrogen per hectare every year.
● The **berries are among the most nutritious fruits**. Concentration of vitamins A, B2 and C of the berries is much higher than other fruits and vegetables such as orange, carrot and tomato. Its vitamin C content is higher than many other vitamin rich fruits like amla, orange, kiwi etc. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Interesting facts about Sea buckthorn:**
● It has been used in the **traditional 'Amchi' system of medicine** for centuries in Ladakh. Even today Amchies (local traditional doctors) often prescribe preparations from seabuckthorn fruit, seed, root etc. for treatment of common problems like wound healing, blood purification, gastric ulcer etc.
● **There are separate male and female plants.** Only the female plants bear yellow, orange or red berries that ripen in August-September.
● Besides the cold and drought resistance, **the shrub is among the first plant** **species that can grow in any barren land.**
**Relevance:** Seeds of seabuckthorn, Himalayan buckwheat will be a part of experiment on ISS.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Places in News and Geopolitical Hotspots (Ukraine, Taiwan Strait, Hormuz, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 Senegal
II. 	 Mauritania
III. 	Mali
IV. 	 Niger
How many of the above countries are part of the
Sahel region?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Sahel**, meaning “the shore” in Arabic, is a vast area crossing from East to West Africa. It covers many geographic and agro-ecological systems, 12 countries and is home to 400 million people.
● It is a **semiarid region of western and north-central Africa.**
● It forms a **transitional zone** between the arid Sahara (desert) to the north and the belt of humid savannas to the south.
● It runs through portions of the countries of **Senegal**, **Mauritania**, **Mali**, Burkina Faso, **Niger**. **So, points I, II, III** **and IV are correct.**
● The vegetation is chiefly of the savanna type, with little continuous cover. It has low-growing grass, thorny shrubs, and scattered acacia and baobab trees. **So, all four of the above countries are part of the Sahel region.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Russia has tightened its grip over the Sahel region in West Africa, signing a key nuclear agreement with Niger.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the Leap-1 Mission:
I. 	 It is India’s first commercial space mission.
II. 	 It is a collaborative initiative of India and
France.
III. 	It is proposed to be launched on the
indigenously developed P30 satellite
platform.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **LEAP-1 mission:** Dhruva Space, budding space tech startup from India, is set to launch its first commercial satellite mission (also, **India’s first commercial space mission**) LEAP-1, marking a pivotal transition from technology demonstrations to customer-driven satellite deployments. **So, statement I is** **correct.**
● The mission, scheduled aboard SpaceX s Falcon 9 in Q3 2025, exemplifies significant **Indo-Australian** **collaboration with growing US support**, underlining Dhruva Space’s expanding global commercial presence. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● LEAP-1 utilises Dhruva Space’s **indigenously developed P-30 satellite platform**, successfully spacequalified during the LEAP-TD mission on ISRO’s PSLV-C58 in January 2024. **So, statement III is** **correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:**  India's Dhruva Space will launch Leap-1 satellite mission aboard SpaceX Falcon-9.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Aquatic Ecosystem and Life (Aquatic Ecosystem, Maritime Zones, Corals, Mangrove, Marine Vegetation)
questions.questionText:
The world is experiencing a fourth mass coral
bleaching event. In the above context, which one
of the following is a prerequisite to make a final
determination of a mass bleaching event?

options.a:
It must be confirmed in each of the major
ocean basins.
options.b:
It must affect at least half of the world’s coral
reefs.
options.c:
It must be reported in both tropical and
temperate seas.
options.d:
It must last for a minimum of two consecutive
years.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The world is currently undergoing its **fourth global coral bleaching event**, as confirmed by the **National** **Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA)** and the **International Coral Reef Initiative** **(ICRI),** marking the second such event in the last decade.
● Prolonged anomalous ocean temperatures, tracked by NOAA’s Coral Reef Watch (CRW), have triggered widespread bleaching across the **Atlantic, Pacific and Indian Oceans since early 2023**. Mass bleaching has been reported in at least **53 countries and territories,** including the U.S. (Florida), the Caribbean, Eastern Tropical Pacific (Mexico to Colombia), Australia’s Great Barrier Reef, South Pacific islands (Fiji, Vanuatu, Tuvalu, Kiribati, Samoas), the Red Sea, Persian Gulf and Gulf of Aden.
● A **mass coral bleaching event** is confirmed **only when bleaching is observed in all major ocean basins.** This condition is now fulfilled, with bleaching reported in the Western Indian Ocean (Tanzania, Kenya, Seychelles, Mauritius, Mayotte, Tromelin, western Indonesia) and the South Atlantic (northeast Brazil).” **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, Western Australia’s coral reefs have been facing their worst recorded bleaching event, triggered by a prolonged and widespread marine heatwave.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 It is the largest of the surviving lemur species.
II. 	 It is endemic to the island of Madagascar.
III. 	It is mainly arboreal in nature.
Which is this species?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Indris (Indri indri) are endemic to the island of** **Madagascar**. This large island’s unique geography makes it a vibrant tapestry of diverse climates and distinct environments. Indris make their homes in the lush tropical rainforests growing along this island’s eastern coast. Here, the land slopes down from the mountainous central highlands toward the coast. Indris generally prefer forests at low-to-mid elevations, but groups are occasionally found at higher altitudes.
● **Indris are the largest lemurs in the world**. Their stubby tails—remnants of their last common ancestor with monkeys, are unique among lemurs. Other species tend to have tails closer to the length of their bodies.
● In the wild, indris live between 15 and 18 years, and they do not survive for long in captivity. Indris are **herbivorous** and eat a variety of plants and plant parts, including fruits, seeds, flowers, and sometimes bark. **Indris spend much of their life in** **the trees but are not strictly arboreal.**
● The indri is classified as **Critically Endangered** by the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN, 2018), appearing on the IUCN Red List of Threatened Species. The indri is listed in the Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species **(CITES) Appendix I.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** A research has, for the first time, ascertained the composition of the intestinal microbiome of the indri, a critically endangered lemur.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.questionText:
Which of the following are classified as creamy
layer in India?
I. 	 Children of parents who hold constitutional
posts
II. 	 Children of parents employed in Group A
services of the Union or All India Services
III. 	Children of parents whose gross annual
income is more than ₹8 lakh, including
income from agricultural land
Select the correct answer using the code given
below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In the **Indra Sawhney vs Union of India (1992) judgement**, the Supreme Court upheld the government’s decision to implement the recommendations of the Mandal Commission, but said that **affluent sections** **among the socially and educationally backward classes, the “creamy layer”**, must be excluded from job quotas.
○ Thereafter, in 1993, DoPT issued a circular identifying the creamy layer that would be **ineligible** **for OBC reservation**. Sons and daughters of **high constitutional functionaries, and** **government**, PSU, and armed forces officers were included in the list, along with “professional class and those engaged in trade and industry”, and “property owners”. An “income/ wealth test” was also mentioned. **So, point I is correct.** ○ Specifically, an individual either of whose parents was a direct recruit to a **Group A/ Class I** **government job**, or if the parent was promoted to Group A before the age of 40, was not eligible for the OBC quota. **So, point II is correct.** ○ An individual both of whose **parents were direct recruits to Group B jobs** would be part of the creamy layer. Children of **armed forces officials** only up to the rank of Lieutenant Colonel could avail of the quota. ○ For those outside the government sector, the income ceiling was set at Rs 1 lakh per annum. It was subsequently revised upwards, and the **limit has been Rs 8 lakh since 2017**. However, **income** **from salary and agricultural income is not included. So, point III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Supreme Court will consider introducing a "creamy layer" system for Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes, similar to the one for OBCs, to exclude more affluent individuals from reservation benefits.
**Points to Remember** The name “indri” is technically a misnomer, and the true Malagasy word for these animals is “babakoto,” a word with various meanings but often translated as ancestor. From October through December, indris tend to stay at lower levels of the canopy in order to avoid horseflies.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 Improve water retention capacity of soils
II. 	 Adsorb CO₂ from industrial exhaust gases
III. 	Increase heat resistance of buildings
IV. 	 Treatment of wastewater
How many of the above are the potential benefits
of biochar?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Biochar** is a type of charcoal rich in carbon and is produced from agricultural residue and organic municipal solid waste. It offers a sustainable alternative to manage waste and capture carbon.
● It can hold carbon in the soil for 100-1,000 years due to its strong and stable characteristics, making it an **effective long-term carbon sink**. Its application across different sectors provides scalable opportunities for reducing emissions.
○ In agriculture, applying biochar **can improve water retention, particularly in semi-dry and** **nutrient-depleted soils**. This, in turn, can abate nitrous oxide emissions by 30-50%. Notably, nitrous oxide is a greenhouse gas with 273-times the warming potential of CO2, making its mitigation a crucial benefit of biochar. **So, point I is correct.** ○ In carbon capture applications, **modified biochar can adsorb CO₂ from industrial exhaust** **gases**. However, its carbon removal efficiency is currently lower than that of conventional methods. **So, point II is correct.** ○ In the construction sector, biochar can be explored as a low-carbon alternative to building materials. Adding **2-5% of biochar to concrete can improve mechanical strength, increase** **heat resistance by 20%**, and capture 115 kg of CO₂ per cubic metre, making building materials a stable carbon sink. **So, point III is correct.** ○ In wastewater treatment, biochar offers a low-cost and effective option to reduce pollution. India generates more than 70 billion litres of wastewater every day, of which 72% is left untreated. **A** **kilogram of biochar, along with other substances, can treat 200-500 litres of wastewater**, implying a biochar demand potential of 2.5-6.3 million tonnes. **So, point IV is correct.** **So, all four of the above are the potential benefits of biochar.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** With the Indian carbon market set to be launched in 2026, CO2 removal technologies such as biochar are expected to play a crucial role.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
The ‘Hyblaea puera Nucleopolyhedrosis Virus
(HpNPV)’ was recently seen in the media in the
context of which one of the following?

options.a: It prevents massive defoliation in teak crops.
options.b:
It promotes faster growth of crops in semi
arid regions.
options.c:
It increases the water retention capacity of
the crops.
options.d:
It decreases the drought resistant capacity of
the crops.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Hyblaea puera Nucleopolyhedrosis Virus (HpNPV)** causes lethal infection in the pest larvae and **prevents widespread defoliation of teak trees.**
● The teak defoliator strips entire teak plantations of their foliage up to six times a year, weakening trees and causing significant loss in timber yield. When the larvae attack, the tree spends its energy regenerating leaves instead of growing.
● HpNPV, screened by the **Kerala Forest Research Institute (KFRI)** is a virus that is **entirely host-** **specific**, targeting only the teak defoliator larvae, and thus completely safe for the forest ecosystem.
○ It **multiplies at least a trillion times inside a single larva**. When the body breaks open, it releases huge quantities of inoculum. Even if the infection is sub-lethal, the virus stays in the insect, gets passed on to the next generation, and proves lethal then. ○ With a clear understanding of the pest’s life cycle, **HpNPV can be applied during early-stage** **outbreaks to prevent larger infestations.**
● Many countries cultivating teak have banned chemical pesticide use. This innovation opens up a massive export potential for the eco-friendly HpNPV technology. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Kerala Forest Research Institute (KFRI) has successfully identified Hyblaea puera Nucleopolyhedrosis Virus (HpNPV), which prevents widespread defoliation of teak trees.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Aquatic Ecosystem and Life (Aquatic Ecosystem, Maritime Zones, Corals, Mangrove, Marine Vegetation)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the Great Barrier Reef:
I. 	 It contains the world’s largest collection of
coral reefs.
II. 	 It is located on the south-west coast of
Australia.
III. 	It is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Great Barrier Reef** is a site of remarkable variety and beauty on the north-east coast of Australia. It contains the **world’s largest** **collection of coral reefs**. It also holds great scientific interest as the habitat of species such as the dugong (‘sea cow’) and the large green turtle, which are threatened with extinction. **So, statement I is** **correct.**
● As the world’s most extensive coral reef ecosystem, the Great Barrier Reef is a globally outstanding and significant entity. Practically **the entire ecosystem was inscribed as UNESCO** **World Heritage in 1981**. It includes extensive cross-shelf diversity, stretching from the low water mark along the mainland coast up to 250 kilometres offshore. **So, statement III is correct.**
● The reef is located off the coast of Queensland, Australia, in the **Coral Sea**. It is a sea of the **southwestern Pacific Ocean**, extending east of Australia and New Guinea, west of New Caledonia and the New Hebrides, and south of the Solomon Islands. **So, statement II** **is not correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Great Barrier Reef has suffered its sharpest coral loss in 39 years, with hard coral cover plunging over 70% in some areas.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
Hepatitis D:
I. 	 It only affects people who already have
Hepatitis B.
II. 	 It has been classified as cancerous by the
World Health Organisation (WHO).
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Hepatitis D is a serious liver infection** caused by the Hepatitis D virus (**HDV**). However, it is an incompetent virus that **requires Hepatitis B virus (HBV) for replication**. This means it cannot cause infection alone and triggers co-infections only with the hepatitis B virus. So hepatitis D **only affects** **people who already have Hepatitis B** either simultaneously or sequentially. **So, statement I is correct.**
○ Hepatitis B & D co-infection runs a more severe course for risk of cirrhosis and liver cancer. **Hepatitis B can cause liver cancer even without causing cirrhosis** as it gets integrated in the DNA of cells. Hepatitis D virus augments the carcinogenic effect of Hepatitis B.
● All types of hepatitis are associated with acute liver infection; however, **only Hepatitis B, C, and D can** **lead to chronic infections** with a higher risk of liver cirrhosis, failure or cancer. According to the authors of the study’s findings, hepatitis D is associated with a two- to six-fold higher risk of liver cancer compared with hepatitis B.
● The World Health Organisation (**WHO**) recently announced the reclassification of **hepatitis D as** **cancerous,** emphasising the urgency for preventing viral hepatitis, which is a growing public health crisis. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The World Health Organisation (WHO) recently announced the reclassification of hepatitis D as cancerous.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText: What is Ectopic Pregnancy?

options.a:
A pregnancy in which the fertilised egg
implants itself outside the uterus
options.b:
A pregnancy in which the foetus exhibits an
abnormal growth in the uterus
options.c:
A pregnancy in which triplets are born
prematurely
options.d:
A pregnancy in which assisted reproductive
technologies are involved

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● An **ectopic pregnancy** occurs when the fertilised egg, instead of implanting itself in the uterus, **implants** **outside of it, commonly in the fallopian tube**. This sort of pregnancy cannot proceed normally. It could cause bleeding, and may even be life-threatening in some cases.
● Pregnancy begins with fertilisation of a sperm cell and and egg cell. The fertilised egg is then meant to travel down the fallopian tubes, which are tubes that connect the ovaries to the uterus, and attach itself to the lining of the uterus (womb). In an ectopic pregnancy, the fertilised egg attaches itself to a structure other than the uterus. When it attaches to the fallopian tubes, it is called a tubal pregnancy. Rarely, **the** **fertilised egg may attach to the ovary, abdominal cavity or cervix**. Since only the uterus is meant to carry a growing embryo, these pregnancies cannot continue.
● Ectopic pregnancies occur when the **movement of the fertilised egg is blocked.** This could occur due to inflammation or scarring of the fallopian tubes, damage to the fallopian tubes (due to prior surgeries or infections), or an irregularly-shaped fallopian tube. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, an MRI scan revealed a foetus growing in the liver.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Places in News and Geopolitical Hotspots (Ukraine, Taiwan Strait, Hormuz, etc.)
questions.questionText:
The Dardanelles strait connects which of the
following water bodies?

options.b: Aegean Sea and Sea of Marmara

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Dardanelles is a strait, or narrow body of water, that **connects the Aegean Sea** **and the Sea of Marmara in Turkey**. It also **separates the continent of Europe** **from the westernmost tip of Asia Minor**. The strait is **named for the ancient city of** **Dardanus**. In ancient times it was called the Hellespont, meaning “Helle’s sea,” in memory of Helle, a mythical princess.
● Another strait, called the Bosporus, connects the Sea of Marmara with the Black Sea. Together the **Bosporus and the** **Dardanelles provide the only sea connection between the lands lying on the Black Sea and the rest** **of the world**. The Gallipoli Peninsula lies along the western side of the strait. Major ports along its shores include Gallipoli, Eceabat, and Çanakkale, all in Turkey. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Dardanelles Strait has been temporarily shut to maritime traffic due to forest fires raging in northwestern Turkey.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: National Park, Wildlife Sanctuary, Biosphere Reserve
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Protected Area Part of Biosphere Reserve
I. - Biligiri Rangaswamy Temple Tiger Reserve
Agasthyamala
II. - Bandipur Tiger Reserve
Nilgiri
III. Mudumalai Wildlife
Sanctuary
Seshachalam
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Biligiri Rangaswamy Temple Tiger Reserve (BRT)** is located in **Karnataka**. This unique Biogeographical habitat is in the **middle of the bridge between Western and Eastern ghats** in South India. The tiger reserve derives its name from 'BILIGIRI ', the white rocky cliff which has a temple of Lord 'Vishnu' locally known as ‘Rangaswamy’.
○ Agasthyamala Biosphere Reserve spans across **Kerala and Tamil Nadu** and is located in the Western Ghats. Consisting mostly of tropical forest, the site is a unique genetic reservoir of cultivated plants, in particular cardamom, jamune, nutmeg, pepper and plantain. Three wildlife sanctuaries, **Shendurney**, **Peppara** and **Neyyar**, are located in the site, as well as the Kalakad Mundanthurai Tiger reserve. **So, pair I is not correctly matched.**
● **Bandipur Tiger Reserve**, located in **Karnataka**, is one of India’s premier wildlife sanctuaries and an essential **component of the Nilgiri Biosphere Reserve**. It forms a contiguous landscape with the **Mudumalai Tiger Reserve** (Tamil Nadu) **Sathyamangalam Tiger Reserve** (Tamil Nadu), **Wayanad** **Wildlife Sanctuary** (Kerala), and **Nagarahole Tiger Reserve** (Karnataka). This combined forest tract is **among the largest continuous habitats for the tiger** (Panthera tigris tigris) and the Asian elephant (Elephas maximus) in the world. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
○ The landscape is diverse, comprising a **mosaic of dry deciduous, moist deciduous, and scrub** **forests**, with the Western Ghats providing ecological richness. The **Kabini**, **Nugu**, and **Moyar** rivers act as crucial water sources, supporting both wildlife and vegetation. The reserve **falls** **within the Kaveri River Basin** and is known for its scenic terrain, rolling hills, and dry valleys.
● **Mudumalai Tiger Reserve** is **located in the Nilgiris District of Tamil Nadu** spread over at the trijunction of three states, viz, **Karnataka, Kerala and Tamil Nadu** and it plays an unique role by forming **part of the Nilgiris Biosphere Reserve**, the first Biosphere Reserve in India, declared during 1986. **So,** **pair III is not correctly matched.**
○ It has a common boundary with **Wayanad Wildlife Sanctuary** (Kerala) on the West, **Bandipur** **Tiger Reserve** (Karnataka) on the North, and the **Nilgiris Division** on the South and East and **Gudalur Forest Division** on the South West, together forming a large conservation landscape for flagship species such as **Tiger and Asian Elephant.**
● The **Seshachalam Hills Biosphere Reserve** is located in **Andhra Pradesh** and is notable for its rich biodiversity, within the Eastern Ghats. This area serves as a crucial habitat for many endemic species and has played a significant role in conservation since its recognition under **UNESCO's Man and Biosphere** **(MAB) Programme** in 2010. **So, only one of the pairs given above is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Notice was issued to religious bodies over Biligiri Rangaswamy Temple Tiger Reserve (BRT) encroachments.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
‘Dhirio’, a traditional form of bull fighting, is
practised in which one of the following states of India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Dhirio**, also called **dhiri**, is a traditional form of **bull fighting in Goa** that has been part of the state’s cultural fabric for generations. **It was practised when the** economy was largely agrarian, it became  a custom to organise such fights **after the harvest season**. **Bull fights** in Goa have been a **generational** **tradition**, dating back to the **Portuguese era**. Unlike the Spanish version, dhirio involves **two trained** **bulls** fighting each other in paddy fields or football grounds, without matadors and without the necessity of killing the animal.
● The bulls charge, lock horns, and push until one either **falls or flees**, marking the end of the fight. Traditionally, these events were part of **church feasts** and large gatherings, drawing thousands of spectators and even political dignitaries.
● The practice was banned in **1996** after a fatal incident led the Bombay High Court at Goa to rule bullfights illegal under the **Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act, 1960**, directing a complete ban on such events. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** In Goa, legislators across party lines recently demanded the legalisation of bull fighting (locally called Dhirio/Dhiri), highlighting it as an integral part of the state’s cultural heritage.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Biodiversity (IUCN, WWF, CBD, Cartagena, Nagoya, etc.)
questions.questionText:
The Equator Prize, also known as the Nobel Prize
for Biodiversity Conservation, is awarded by
which one of the following organisations?

options.b:
International Union for Conservation of
Nature (IUCN)

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Equator Prize**, organized by the **Equator Initiative within the United Nations Development** **Programme (UNDP)**, is awarded **biennially** to recognize outstanding community **efforts to reduce** **poverty through the conservation and sustainable use of biodiversity**.
● To be eligible for the Equator Prize, the following requirements apply: ○ The initiative **must have been in existence for at least three years**, and/or the actions taken must have been in place for at least three years. ○ The nominee must be either **a local community-based group**, operating in a rural area, based in **a country receiving support from the UNDP** or an Indigenous Peoples’ community in any country, operating in a rural area. ○ The actions taken must be nature-based, and **must deliver benefits related to two or more** **Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)**.
● **Bibi Fatima Women’s SHG is the sole group from India to win the award**, other winners are from Argentina, Brazil, Ecuador, Indonesia, Kenya, Papua New Guinea, Peru, and Tanzania.
● The award includes a **cash prize of $10,000 (approximately ₹8.5 lakh)**. This year’s award selection was based on the theme “Women and Youth Leadership for Nature-Based Climate Action”. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** A self-help group (SHG) from a small village in Kundgol taluk of Dharwad district has bagged the ‘Equator Initiative Award’.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard
to the State Health Regulatory Excellence Index (SHRESTH):
I.  	 It will categorise the States and the Union
Territories as ‘manufacturing states’ and ‘primarily distribution states/UTs’ for ranking purposes.
II. 	 It will be implemented by the Central Drugs
Standard Control Organization (CDSCO).
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The State Health Regulatory Excellence Index (**SHRESTH**) is a **first-of-its-kind national initiative** to benchmark and strengthen state drug regulatory systems through a transparent, data-driven framework.
● It has been **proposed by the Central Drugs Standard Control Organization (CDSCO)**. It aims to improve the performance of state drug regulatory authorities nationwide, guaranteeing that drug safety and quality standards are consistently met. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The new index will **enable targeted improvements in human resources**, infrastructure, and digitization across states, ensuring drug safety is guaranteed for every Indian, regardless of where they live.
○ The **states will be divided into two categories:** manufacturing states  and primarily distribution states/UTs’, and will be ranked accordingly. Manufacturing states will be evaluated on 27 indices across five key areas: human resources, infrastructure, licensing activities, surveillance activities, and responsiveness. Primarily distribution states will be evaluated on 23 indices. **So, statement I** **is correct.**
● The **CDSCO will facilitate the sharing of success stories from top-performing states** to encourage collaboration and the transfer of knowledge.
● The SHRESTH is not just a scorecard but a roadmap for states to ensure safe and effective drugs and medical devices. A uniform implementation of the Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940 along with the sharing of best practices and a structured knowledge-sharing platform, will help ensure that all states have a functional regulatory system. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Union Health Secretary virtually launched the State Health Regulatory Excellence Index (SHRESTH).

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. Cash payments
II. Bank transfers
III. Standing orders
IV. UPI AutoPay
V. Cheque payments
How many of the above are 'pull transactions'?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Push and pull payments** are two distinct methods of initiating transactions, mainly differing in who controls the flow of money.
○ In push payments, the payer has complete control over how much money is sent, when it is sent and to whom. Common examples include **cash payments**, where the payer directly hands over the money, **bank transfers,** where the payer decides the amount and destination and **standing orders**,
which are scheduled transfers set up in advance but still initiated by the payer. **So, points I, II and** **III are not correct.** ○ **Pull payments** are controlled by the **payee or the business receiving the money.** Here, the payer gives prior authorisation and the payee withdraws funds as agreed. **UPI Autopay** which involves direct debit is a clear example, as the **payee collects money from the payer’s account at** **scheduled intervals**. **So, point IV is correct.**
■ A **UPI pull transaction,** also called a collect request, is a type of payment where the **recipient starts the transaction instead of the sender**. Once the payment request is initiated, the sender approves it by entering their UPI PIN. ○ Similarly, **cheques** fall under pull payments; even though the payer writes the cheque, it merely acts as permission for the payee to initiate the actual transfer of funds. **So, point V is correct.** **So, only two of the above are pull transactions.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, the National Payment Corporation of India has announced that it will discontinue the pull transactions on the UPI platform.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Biodiversity (IUCN, WWF, CBD, Cartagena, Nagoya, etc.)
questions.questionText:
The International Day of the World's Indigenous
Peoples (World Tribal Day) is observed on August 9 each year to raise awareness and protect the rights of the world's indigenous population. In the above context, consider the following statements:
I.  	 It is declared by the United Nations
Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO).
II. 	 The theme for 2025 is ‘Indigenous Peoples
and Artificial Intelligence – Defending Rights, Shaping the Future’.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **International Day of the World’s Indigenous Peoples** takes place annually on **9 August** to celebrate Indigenous Peoples and their knowledge and raise awareness of the needs of these groups. The Day highlights the **rights of Indigenous Peoples to make their own decisions** and carry them out in ways that are meaningful and culturally appropriate to them.
● The **UN General Assembly (UNGA) decided to observe the International Day of the World’s** **Indigenous People** annually, on 9 August. The date represents the first meeting, in 1982, of the UN Working Group on Indigenous Populations. **So, statement I is not correct.**
○ There are an estimated 476 million Indigenous Peoples in the world. They make up less than 6% of the world’s population but a**ccount for at least 15% of the world’s poorest**. Indigenous Peoples represent over 5,000 different cultures in more than 90 countries.
● The theme for 2025 is Indigenous Peoples and Artificial Intelligence – Defending Rights, Shaping the Future. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** World Tribal Day was observed on August 9.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Hydropower Project River
I. - Sawalkote Beas
II. - Tato II Siyom
III. - Seli Chenab
IV. - Miar Satluj
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Sawalkote Hydro Project** is designed as a run-of-the-river scheme and will feature a 192.5-metrehigh Roller Compacted Concrete Gravity Dam, horseshoe-shaped diversion tunnels, and an underground power house **on the left bank of the Chenab**. **So, pair I is not correctly matched.**
○ Construction of the Sawalkote project is **a major strategic development aimed at optimising** **India’s use of Indus waters** while the treaty with Pakistan remains in abeyance.
● The **Tato-II Hydro Electric Project (HEP)** will be constructed in the Shi Yomi District of **Arunachal** **Pradesh**. The Power generated from the Project will help improve the power supply position in the state of Arunachal Pradesh and will also help in balancing the national Grid. It will be constructed on the banks of **River Siyom**. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● The **Seli and Miyar HEPs are run of river (RoR) projects located in Chenab Basin** at Lahaul and Spiti districts in the state of **Himachal Pradesh**. Himachal Pradesh and Telangana government have signed an MoU for the Seli and Miyar hydropower projects on the Chenab River. Recently there have been oppositions against these projects in the Lahaul region. **So, pair III is correctly matched and pair IV is** **not correctly matched.** **So, only two of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Cabinet Committee on Economic Affairs has sanctioned the construction of the 700 MW TatoII Hydro-Electric Project in Arunachal Pradesh.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the Semicon India Programme:
I. 	 It extends a fiscal support of 100% of the
project cost for setting up of Silicon CMOS based Semiconductor Fabs in India.
II. 	 It provides a Product Design Linked
Incentive of up to 50% of the eligible expenditure subject to a ceiling of ₹15 crore per application.
III. 	It is implemented under the India
Semiconductor Mission (ISM).
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Semicon India programme** aims to provide financial support to companies investing in semiconductors, display manufacturing and design ecosystem. Following four schemes have been introduced under the aforesaid programme:
○ **‘Modified Scheme for setting up of Semiconductor Fabs in India’** extends a fiscal support of **50% of the project cost** on pari-passu (equal) basis for setting up of Silicon CMOS based Semiconductor Fabs in India. **So, statement I is not correct.** ○ **‘Modified Scheme for setting up of Display Fabs in India’** extends fiscal support of 50% of Project Cost on pari-passu basis for setting up of Display Fabs in India.
○ **‘Modified Scheme for setting up of Compound Semiconductors** / Silicon Photonics / Sensors Fab / Discrete Semiconductors Fab and Semiconductor Assembly, Testing, Marking and Packaging (ATMP) / OSAT facilities in India’ extends a fiscal support of 50% of the Capital Expenditure on pari-passu basis for setting up of Compound Semiconductors / Silicon Photonics (SiPh) / Sensors (including MEMS) Fab/ Discrete Semiconductor Fab and Semiconductor ATMP / OSAT facilities in India. ○ **‘Design Linked Incentive (DLI) Scheme’:** In addition to the design infrastructure support, the scheme provides “**Product Design Linked Incentive” of up to 50% of the eligible expenditure** **subject to a ceiling of ₹15 Crore per application** and “Deployment Linked Incentive” of 6% to 4% of net sales turnover over 5 years subject to a ceiling of ₹30 Crore per application. **So,** **statement II is correct.**
● **It is implemented through the India Semiconductor Mission (ISM). So, statement III is correct.** ○ **India Semiconductor Mission (ISM)** is a specialized and independent institution under the Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY), Government of India. ○ It is an independent Business Division within the Digital India Corporation. ○ It aims to build a vibrant semiconductor and display ecosystem to enable India’s emergence as a global hub for electronics manufacturing and design. ○ ISM has all the administrative and financial powers and is tasked with the responsibility of catalysing the India Semiconductor ecosystem in manufacturing, packaging, and design. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The government approved four additional semiconductor manufacturing projects in the states of Odisha, Punjab, and Andhra Pradesh under the India Semiconductor Mission (ISM).

questions.subject: Environment
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the symbiotic relationship in Lichens:
I.  	 It involves a mycobiont and one or more
photobionts.
II.  	 It helps build soil and serves as nature’s
bioindicator.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Lichens** are not just one organism but two (sometimes more) living in **intimate** symbiosis. Despite their humble appearance, lichens play critical roles in ecosystems, **building soil**, feeding insects and serving as nature’s **bioindicators**.  **So, statement II** **is correct.**
● They are formed by a **symbiotic relationship** between a fungus (**mycobiont**) and a photosynthetic partner, which is typically a green alga or cyanobacterium (**photobiont**). **So, statement I is** **correct.**
● The fungus provides the physical structure, shelter, moisture, and habitat, while the photosynthetic partner produces food and nutrients through photosynthesis. This mutualistic partnership results in a **composite organism that can thrive in harsh** **environments where neither partner could survive alone.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Types of Organisms in Lichens:**
● Many unrelated and very different fungi form lichens, including mushroom-forming fungi, and especially cup-fungi. **98% of lichen fungi are cup-fungi**, or ascomycetes. Fully half of all ascomycetes and one in five of all known fungi form lichens. Lichenisation is an ecological strategy, or a common nutritional mode among unrelated fungi.
● The lichen fungi share a common ecological strategy of hosting an internal population of photosynthetic cells, from which they obtain their carbon source in the form of simple sugars. These photosynthetic cells can either be **green algae (Chlorophyta) or** **cyanobacteria or sometimes both**, in which case the cyanobacteria are localised in distinct areas of the thallus. As the photosynthetic partners come from divergent parts of the tree of life (green plants vs bacteria), the term photobiont is used as a collective term for any of them. The majority of lichens have green-algal photobionts, with only about 10% containing cyanobacteria.
**Relevance:** A team of Indian scientists has uncovered a previously unknown species of lichen, Allographa effusosoredica, from the richly biodiverse Western Ghats, carrying within it a tale of symbiosis, evolution and resilience.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
It is a radioactive isotope of hydrogen. It is
produced naturally from interactions of cosmic rays with gases in the upper atmosphere, and is also a by-product of nuclear reactors. When combined with phosphor, it creates glow-in-the-dark lighting such as exit signs, emergency lighting in buildings and airport runway lights. It is:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Tritium is a radioactive isotope of hydrogen**. It has the same number of protons and electrons as hydrogen, but has 2 neutrons, whereas regular hydrogen does not have any. This makes tritium unstable and radioactive.
● Tritium is **produced naturally** from interactions of cosmic rays with gases in the upper atmosphere, and is also a **by-product of nuclear reactors.**
● Tritium can be **combined with phosphor to create glow-in-the-dark lighting** such as exit signs, emergency lighting in buildings, and airport runway lights. Like all radioactive isotopes, tritium decays. As it decays, it emits beta radiation.
● The physical half-life of tritium is 12.33 years, meaning that it takes just over 12 years for tritium to decay to half of its original amount. As tritium decays, it changes to helium.
● It is also **used as a tracer in biomedical research** to study and diagnose heart disease, cancer and AIDS. In the future, it may also be used to generate electricity in fusion reactors.
● The **most common form of tritium is tritiated water**, which is formed when a tritium atom replaces a hydrogen atom in water (H2O) to form HTO. HTO has the same chemical properties as water and is odourless and colourless. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** A recent global ocean model affirms Fukushima wastewater release is safe.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Initiative Feature
I. - MERITE Improve governance of technical education
II. - Bharat Forecast System
Predict small-scale weather features accurately
III. CATCH
Grant Program
AI solutions to strengthen cancer screening
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **MERITE** is a **Central Sector Scheme** designed to **elevate the quality, equity, and governance of** **technical education nationwide**. This ambitious programme aligns closely with the National Education Policy (**NEP**) 2020’s vision for a multidisciplinary, innovative, and skill-driven education system. It will be **rolled out over five years**, from 2025-26 to 2029-30. From the budget of total Rs 4200 crores half of it which is Rs 2100 crores will come **from the World Bank as a loan** highlighting the global confidence in India’s educational reforms. The rest will be funded by the Central Government. Additionally, state and UT departments managing technical education will receive support. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
○ The scheme will impact around 275 government and government-aided technical institutions including:
■ National Institutes of Technology (NITs) ■ State Engineering Colleges ■ Polytechnics ■ Affiliating Technical Universities (ATUs)
● **Bharat Forecasting System (BFS)** is developed by the Pune-based Indian Institute of Tropical Meteorology. It will **provide forecasts with a 6-kilometer resolution-the highest in the world**-which will allow the meteorological department to predict small-scale weather features more accurately. **So, pair** **II is correctly matched.**
○ The system will **boost monsoon tracking**, aviation, cyclone and disaster management, agriculture, waterways, defence, flood forecasting, and also support key ministries. ○ The BFS , which will be **operational up to the Panchayat level**, is expected to help address extreme rainfall events, among other things. The new model allows a horizontal resolution of about 6 km, which is typically the size of a cluster or panchayat. ○ The Bharat FS is the **only global numerical weather prediction system in the world** that is deployed at such a high resolution, representing a major advancement in India’s forecasting capabilities.
● The **Cancer AI & Technology Challenge (CATCH) Grant Program** was launched by the IndiaAI Independent Business Division (IBD), in collaboration with the National Cancer Grid (NCG). This initiative seeks to support the development and deployment of **innovative Artificial Intelligence (AI)** **solutions to strengthen cancer screening**, diagnostics, treatment support, and healthcare operations across India. **So, pair III is correctly matched.**
○ The CATCH Grant Program will **provide up to ₹50 lakh per project to selected teams** comprising technology innovators and clinical institutions. The grants will be **co-funded by** **IndiaAI and NCG**. ○ The initiative is designed to catalyse piloted deployment of AI solutions within the NCG hospital network, with the potential for future scale-up based on demonstrated clinical impact and operational readiness. **So, all three of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Union Cabinet has approved a proposal to implement the Multidisciplinary Education and Research Improvement in Technical Education (MERITE) scheme in 275 institutions.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
The ‘Notch signaling pathway’ in animals is
primarily involved in:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Notch signaling pathway** is a highly conserved, intercellular signaling mechanism essential for proper embryonic development in all metazoan organisms in the Animal kingdom. It is important for **communication between adjacent cells to regulate cell development.**
● It mainly consists of three parts: Notch receptor, Notch ligand (DSL protein) and intracellular effector molecule (CSL-DNA binding protein).
● Mammals possess four different notch receptors, referred to as NOTCH1, NOTCH2, NOTCH3, and NOTCH4. Their primary function is to **bind to the ligand and initiate the Notch signal**. The Notch proteins (Notch1-Notch4 in vertebrates) are single-pass receptors that are activated by the Delta (or Deltalike) and Jagged/Serrate families of membrane-bound ligands. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** A recent study published in the in Cell highlights how researchers have designed the AI based protein that activates Notch signaling pathway to boost T-cell production.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Global Groupings and Agreements (FTA, Paris Agreement, NPT, QUAD, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Which of the following countries are the members
of the BIMSTEC?
I. 	 Thailand
II. 	 Vietnam
III.	 Myanmar
IV. 	 Malaysia
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Bay of Bengal Initiative for Multi-Sectoral** **Technical and Economic Cooperation (BIMSTEC)** is a grouping of seven Member States lying in the littoral and adjacent areas of the Bay of Bengal. It is a unique link **connecting South Asia with South-East Asia** - five Members from South Asia (Bangladesh, Bhutan, India, Nepal and Sri Lanka) and **two from South-East Asia** **(Myanmar and Thailand)**. The BIMSTEC region brings together 1.7 billion people - 22% of world population with a combined GDP of US$ 5 trillion. **So, points I and III** **are correct.**
● The organization came into existence in **1997 through the** **'Bangkok Declaration'**. It was originally formed with four Member States with the acronym 'BIST-EC' (Bangladesh, India, Sri Lanka and Thailand Economic Cooperation). Following the inclusion of Myanmar in December 1997, it was renamed 'BIMST-EC' (Bangladesh, India, Myanmar, Sri Lanka and Thailand Economic Cooperation). With the inclusion of Nepal and Bhutan in 2004, the name of the grouping was changed to BIMSTEC.
● BIMSTEC organizes **inter-governmental interactions** through Summits, Ministerial Meetings, Senior Officials Meetings, Permanent Working Committee, Joint Working Groups and Experts level Meetings.
● Vietnam and Malaysia are not the members of BIMSTEC. **So, points II and IV are not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The first-ever BIMSTEC Traditional Music Festival was held at Bharat Mandapam in New Delhi.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Financial Markets (Money Market, Capital Market, SEBI)
questions.questionText: ‘Framework for Responsible and Ethical Enablement of Artificial Intelligence (FREE-AI)’ in the financial sector has been established by:

options.c:
Securities and Exchange Board of India
(SEBI)

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Reserve Bank of India (RBI)** established a committee in 2023 to develop the **Framework for** **Responsible and Ethical Enablement of Artificial Intelligence (FREE-AI)** in the financial sector. The committee submitted **26 recommendations**, built on six foundational pillars including infrastructure, policy, capacity, governance, protection and assurance.
○ It emphasised the need to build a high-quality financial sector data infrastructure as part of the **national digital public infrastructure**, enabling the development of trustworthy AI models for financial applications. ○ This infrastructure could be integrated with **AI Kosh** – the national dataset platform under the IndiaAI Mission. ○ The committee also highlighted the importance of data lifecycle governance, consumer protection and cybersecurity safeguards to ensure AI adoption remains responsible, ethical and transparent. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) has published the report of a committee on the Framework for Responsible and Ethical Enablement of Artificial Intelligence (FREE-AI) for the financial sector.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Recently, the Government of India has made certain
regulations mandatory for the manufacturing of wind turbines in the country. Which of the following are included in it?
I. 	 Sourcing of 100% wind turbine components
from domestic suppliers
II. 	 Storing of all wind turbine data within India
III. 	Setting up of Research & Development
centres by all manufacturers in India within one year
IV. 	 Certification of wind turbines by the Bureau
of Indian Standards (BIS)
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● India’s **wind energy sector** is undergoing a major transformation with new regulations that came into force in July 2025. The **Ministry of New and Renewable Energy** has replaced the **Revised List of** **Models and Manufacturers (RLMM)** with the **Approved List of Models and Manufacturers** **(ALMM-Wind)**, introducing stringent domestic requirements.
● The following are the key features of the framework: ○ **Domestic Supply Chains:** Manufacturers must source all key turbine components, including blades, towers, gearboxes, generators and special bearings, exclusively from approved Indian suppliers. These components account for **nearly 65–70% of total turbine cost,** making the mandate highly consequential for market dynamics. **So, point I is not correct.** ○ **Cybersecurity Safeguards:** The regulations mandate that **all wind turbine data be stored within** **India.** Real-time operational data transfers abroad are prohibited and operational control must be handled **only through domestic facilities**, addressing national security concerns. **So, point II is** **correct.** ○ **Research & Development centres within India:** All manufacturers are required to establish **research and development centres in India within one year**, to design turbines suited for local conditions such as low wind speeds and high temperatures. **So, point III is correct.** ○ **Quality and Safety Standards:** From September 2026, **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** **certification** will become mandatory under the broader **Machinery and Electrical Equipment** **Safety Regulations**, ensuring stricter safety and performance compliance. **So, point IV is correct.**
● The policy aims to raise India’s wind manufacturing capacity utilisation from the present **25–30% to an** **ambitious 70–80%,** while simultaneously bolstering energy security, domestic innovation and industrial self-reliance.
**Points to Remember**
● As of June 2025, India’s total installed power capacity has reached a significant milestone with 476 GW, led by 240 GW of thermal, 110.9 GW of solar, and **51.3 GW of wind power,** marking a strong shift towards renewable energy and energy security.
**Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** India’s wind energy sector is undergoing a significant transformation, with new regulations mandating the establishment of domestic supply chains and data centres for wind turbine manufacturing.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
With regard to the toll charges on National
Highways in India, consider the following statements:
I. 	 Perpetual tolling allows toll collection
to continue even after project costs are recovered.
II. 	 Toll rates are revised annually through a
fixed increment and partial linkage with the Wholesale Price Index.
